reasoning: The reference 30-day average over the past 360 days ending Jan 2026 is 53.4. ACLED data shows there was a massive spike of over 120 demonstrations during a short period in September 2025 (involving severe violence, setting fire to Parliament, etc.). This extreme cluster of events significantly elevates the 360-day mean. A rolling average skewed by a single massive outbreak means that typical 'peaceful' or 'normal' months will fall well below this mean. Aside from standard background strikes and small opposition rallies, there is no evidence of a renewed mass mobilization or constitutional crisis ongoing in Sri Lanka right now (January 2026). Dissanayake's new government took power recently and major widespread violence is not being currently reported. Therefore, the number of protests in the 30 days prior to Jan 25, 2026 and Feb 17, 2026 is highly likely to be lower than the artificially high 53.4 average. Since this relies on a mathematical property of long-tailed distributions (the median is lower than the mean) combined with the specific discovery of a massive 2025 spike, I assign a low probability to these future 30-day periods surpassing the 360-day average. I increase uncertainty slightly for the second date as unexpected events could always occur.
